title: Smoked haddock chowder
subtitle: Naturally smoked haddock with potato, leek, and cream
excerpt: Naturally smoked haddock poached in milk, flaked into a simple potato and leek base, finished with cream. A British chowder that is closer to the Scottish cullen skink than to the American original.
sourceNotes: Smoked haddock chowder draws on the Scottish cullen skink tradition (documented in Theodora FitzGibbon, A Taste of Scotland, 1970) and the broader British tradition of milk-poached smoked fish soups. Cullen skink is the most famous British smoked haddock soup; this version is a chowder variant with the addition of leeks.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The base can be made a day ahead without the fish and cream. Add these when reheating.

content.text: The smoked haddock must be naturally smoked, not dyed. The bright yellow fillets dyed with tartrazine taste chemically and turn the milk an unpleasant colour. Naturally smoked haddock is cream or pale gold and available from good fishmongers and many supermarkets; it produces a broth with a proper smoky sweetness. Finnan haddie, a cold-smoked Scottish variety, is the ideal if you can find it.

The milk used to poach the fish becomes the base of the chowder: do not discard it. All the smokiness and fish flavour is in that liquid. Poach the fish gently so it remains in large flakes rather than falling apart into shreds.

items.prepNote: waxy variety, peeled and cut into 1.5 cm cubes
white and pale green parts, finely sliced

content.text: Place the haddock skin-side down in a wide pan. Pour over {{whole-milk}} of cold milk and add the bay leaves. Bring to a very gentle simmer over medium-low heat. Poach for 8 to 10 minutes until the fish flakes easily when pressed. Remove the fish with a slotted spoon and set aside. Reserve all the poaching milk. When cool enough to handle, remove the skin and any bones and flake the fish into large pieces.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over medium-low heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion and {{leek}} sliced leeks for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{potato}} potato cubes and pour over the reserved poaching milk, discarding the bay leaves. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 15 to 18 minutes until the potatoes are tender.

Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream. Gently fold in the flaked haddock and heat through for 2 minutes without stirring too vigorously — the fish should remain in large, distinct flakes. Taste and season with black pepper; the salt from the smoked fish usually means no additional salt is needed. Scatter with chopped parsley and serve with crusty bread.

items.cause: Smoked haddock varied in salt content, or the fish was heavily brined
items.fix: Add an extra splash of milk or cream to dilute. Heavily smoked or brined haddock can be very salty. Check the fillet before cooking: if it is very salt-stiff, soak in cold water for 30 minutes first.

items.cause: Heat was too high during poaching or the final simmer
items.fix: Keep the heat gentle throughout. If the milk splits, whisk vigorously — it will usually re-emulsify in a chowder.

items.symptom: Fish has broken into tiny flakes
items.cause: Overcooked, or stirred too vigorously when adding it back
items.fix: Fold the fish in gently at the end and heat briefly. The fish only needs 2 minutes to warm through.

items.cause: Haddock was dyed rather than naturally smoked
items.fix: Use naturally smoked haddock only. If the fillet is a vivid yellow, it has been dyed and will taste chemical rather than smoky.

content.text: Smoked haddock chowder sits in the same tradition as cullen skink, the Scottish smoked haddock soup from the village of Cullen in Moray, which has been made in its current form since at least the early twentieth century. Cullen skink uses mashed potato to thicken the broth; this chowder version uses cubed potato instead. The British smoked fish soup tradition is distinct from the cream-and-clam American chowder: it relies on the flavour of the smoked fish rather than shellfish, and the milk base gives it a lighter, less rich character.
